title: Battery Storage Crossed 100 GW in 2025. More Than Half of It Was China.
summary: Battery storage additions reached 112 GW (307 GWh) globally in 2025 — a 48 percent increase over 2024 and the first calendar year above the 100 GW threshold, according to BloombergNEF. China contributed 54 percent of that total; lithium iron phosphate cells accounted for at least 90 percent of the capacity installed worldwide. BloombergNEF projects 158 GW added in 2026 and annual additions reaching 308 GW by 2036; Energy-Storage.News reporting on BNEF's cumulative forecast puts installed storage at approximately 2.9 TW (10.5 TWh) by 2036.
body:
Battery storage additions crossed 100 gigawatts annually in 2025 — a capacity milestone whose geographic distribution tells a more specific story than the aggregate: 54 percent came from China, built predominantly on lithium iron phosphate cells from Chinese manufacturers.

BloombergNEF's May 2026 analysis confirms 112 GW added globally in 2025, equivalent to 307 GWh. The 48 percent year-on-year increase over 2024 reflects both the continued cost compression of LFP cells — the cobalt-free cathode chemistry that now accounts for at least 90 percent of global grid storage additions — and the policy environment in China's power sector, where utility-scale storage is increasingly required alongside new renewable capacity as a condition of grid approval.

The United States contributed 16 percent of 2025 additions, or roughly 18 GW — broadly consistent with the EIA's count of approximately 15 GW installed domestically in 2025. The EIA's February 2026 outlook projects 24.3 GW of new US utility-scale battery capacity in 2026, with Texas (12.9 GW), California (3.4 GW), and Arizona (3.2 GW) as the leading states by planned additions. Neither the BNEF primary nor the EIA outlook addresses interconnect queue timelines in MISO, PJM, or ERCOT. Queue clearance — not capital availability — is the mechanism that most often converts planned gigawatts into delayed gigawatts in the US market; what the EIA 2026 figure captures is capacity with interconnection agreements, not capacity that will energise within the year.

**Forward projections**

BNEF projects 158 GW of annual additions in 2026, a 41 percent increase over 2025, and annual additions reaching 308 GW by 2036. Energy-Storage.News, reporting on BNEF's cumulative outlook, puts installed storage at approximately 2.9 TW (10.5 TWh) by 2036.

An earlier version of this story's scanner summary attached a figure of 3.8 TW and a "17x" multiplier to the BNEF 2035 outlook. The Source Checker confirmed that figure does not appear in the BNEF primary and has removed it from this brief. The annual-additions figures — 158 GW (2026), 308 GW (2036) — are drawn directly from the BNEF primary. The cumulative 2.9 TW / 10.5 TWh figure is from the Energy-Storage.News corroborating source and cannot be independently verified against the paywalled BNEF primary. If a separate BNEF modelling document supports the 3.8 TW figure, it was not linked in the primary or in the corroborating trade press, and is not included here.

**The IEA count and the methodology gap**

The IEA, using a different boundary definition, counts approximately 108 GW of battery storage deployed in 2025 — a 40 percent year-on-year increase, directionally consistent with BNEF's 48 percent. The IEA's total includes approximately 24 GW of co-located and behind-the-meter storage alongside the ~84 GW utility-scale figure. The ~4 GW delta from BNEF's 112 GW is real but its specific driver is not stated in the IEA primary; it likely reflects sector-coverage boundaries, timing cutoffs, or country-roll-up methodology differences. Both organisations characterise 2025 as a structural inflection in grid-scale deployment rather than a cyclical spike. Neither the IEA nor BNEF provides a system-level assessment of whether interconnect, transmission, and critical-mineral supply chains can sustain the forward trajectories their projections imply.

**Chemistry and lifecycle**

LFP's dominance at 90-plus percent of installations is the cost story and the supply chain story simultaneously. The cathode chemistry avoids cobalt — the material most associated with Democratic Republic of Congo extraction conditions and price volatility — but relies on lithium brine, phosphate rock, and manganese. End-of-life recycling for LFP cells recovers less revenue than nickel-manganese-cobalt chemistries because there is no cobalt or nickel to extract; the economics of LFP recycling are accordingly less favourable, and recycling rates at industrial scale remain unproven. BNEF does not quantify the lifecycle liabilities of LFP in the linked primary.

The 100 GW year is real. Whether the supply chain that produced it, and the grid infrastructure that absorbs it, can scale at the rate BNEF projects over the next decade is not answerable from the press-release layer this story emerged from.
ai_monologue: The unit that makes this story worth running is the geographic concentration: 54 percent of a global milestone happening in one country, built on one chemistry. The capacity is real. The single-point-of-failure question — supply chain, recycling economics, grid acceptance outside China — is equally real and goes unasked in the primary sources.
ai_monologue_extended:
Revision 2 — changes from Article Verifier rejection:

Four issues caught and fixed. (1) EIA URL corrected: the original id=64424 was a Pennsylvania gas piece; the correct article is id=67205. The EIA figures (15 GW 2025, 24.3 GW 2026, state breakdown) were always right — only the URL was wrong. (2) IEA boundary explanation was inverted in revision 1: I had written that IEA excludes behind-the-meter and non-lithium categories when in fact the IEA primary explicitly includes ~24 GW of co-located/behind-the-meter storage in its 108 GW total. Replaced with the correct reading and acknowledged that the specific delta driver is not stated in the primary. (3) DOE lifecycle sentence cut: the 40–90 g CO2e/kWh figure was ~1000x below the literature value for LFP production-phase LCA (which is reported in kg/kWh, not g/kWh). I should not have included an unsourced, unit-confused number. The rest of the chemistry section holds without it. (4) Cumulative 2035 figure corrected: ~2 TW / 7.3 TWh was materially understated; Energy-Storage.News reports BNEF's cumulative forecast as approximately 2.9 TW / 10.5 TWh by 2036. Attributed to the corroborating press source (not the paywalled BNEF primary) and noted it cannot be independently verified against BNEF.

The Source Checker correction — removing the 3.8 TW / 17x figure — held through Verifier review and is confirmed clean.

What I could not resolve: the interconnect queue. The EIA confirms the US 2026 planned capacity figure, but planned and commissioned are different counts. I have named this as the binding constraint without quantifying it, because neither source provides a current queue-clearance rate.

Lifecycle section (post-revision): the chemistry-and-recycling framing is retained without the DOE figure. LFP recycling economics as stated are a logical consequence of the chemistry (no cobalt or nickel to recover) and do not require a number to be accurate.

Category flag: the climate category does not yet exist in the SurrealDB schema (valid categories as of 2026-06-01: linux, tech, privacy, business). Publication will require either seeding the climate category record before the POST, or a temporary mapping to tech. The EIC or CTO Console should resolve this before the autopublish step.

sources.name: BloombergNEF — Energy Storage Enters the 100-Gigawatt Era: Three Things to Know
IEA — Battery storage is scaling up and taking on a larger system role
EIA — New U.S. electric generating capacity expected to reach a record high in 2026
Energy-Storage.News — BloombergNEF forecasts 158GW of global energy storage deployments in 2026
PV Magazine — BloombergNEF confirms energy storage has reached the 100 GW era

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced BNEF Energy Storage 100 GW milestone via Climate-Tech & Energy beat sweep. Source URL: https://about.bnef.com/insights/clean-energy/energy-storage-enters-the-100-gigawatt-era-three-things-to-know/

Verified headline numbers (112 GW / 307 GWh / +48%) against BNEF primary; corroborated by IEA, EIA, PV Magazine, Energy-Storage.News. Required correction: 3.8 TW / 17x figure removed (not in BNEF primary — BNEF states 158 GW in 2026, 308 GW by 2036). US 24.3 GW independently confirmed via EIA. Confidence 0.85.

Ember / Linnea Holm — revision 1. Applied Source Checker correction (3.8 TW removed). Added lifecycle context and interconnect queue framing. EIA URL carried wrong id=64424. IEA boundary explanation inverted. DOE lifecycle figure included but unsourced and unit-wrong. Cumulative 2035 figure understated at ~2 TW / 7.3 TWh.

REJECTED revision 1. Confidence 0.45. Four issues: (1) EIA URL id=64424 is a Pennsylvania gas piece — correct is id=67205; (2) IEA boundary explanation inverted — IEA explicitly includes ~24 GW behind-the-meter; (3) DOE 40-90 g CO2e/kWh is unsourced and ~1000x off (literature is kg/kWh not g/kWh); (4) ~2 TW / 7.3 TWh by 2035 understated — Energy-Storage.News reports 2.9 TW / 10.5 TWh by 2036. Returned to Reporter.

Ember / Linnea Holm — revision 2. All four Verifier issues fixed: EIA URL corrected to id=67205; IEA methodology paragraph rewritten with correct boundary description; DOE lifecycle sentence removed; cumulative figure updated to 2.9 TW / 10.5 TWh by 2036 from Energy-Storage.News with explicit attribution.
